How to test for keyboard accessibility

In this blog we'll look at some of the ways you can check keyboard accessibility for your website content.

Why keyboard accessibility is important

People may interact with your website using only a keyboard for many reasons. For example, someone may choose or need to interact with a keyboard because they cannot complete fine motor movements meaning they can't use a mouse or trackpad. Those people with repetitive strain injury (RSI) or who have Parkinson's are just two examples of groups who benefit from a keyboard accessible experience. But, parents who need to hold a baby in one arm are another group who also benefit.

The tests below will also help to find issues for people who interact using keyboard-like inputs, too. For example people using switch devices -- including head wands or eye tracking software. The checks will also help you find issues for people using voice recognition software.

How to do a quick test

A simple but effective test of the accessibility of an interface is to check that any tasks you can complete with a mouse are also available using a keyboard. Test the following, specifically:

  • Tab to move through the interactive elements of the page
  • Shift + Tab to reverse back up to the page through the interactive elements
  • Enter OR Space bar to select button controls
  • Left / Right / Up /Down buttons for radio buttons.

Once you start the previous key press tests, it's also important that you can easily see where you are on the page. Being able to see where you are on a page as a keyboard only user is known as seeing the 'keyboard focus'. This is identified by a 'keyboard focus style' or 'keyboard focus indicator'.

Keyboard focus styles

An accessible interface design includes strong easily discernable focus styles. Essentially, this is about checking whether a broad group of people be able to easily identify visually which element on a web page currently has focus. One simply way to achieve this is to provide a highly contrasting thick border around each interactive elemment -- such as a button, link or form input.

The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) requires the design to use colour which have at least a 3:1 contrast against their adjacent colour.
